title: Managed Identities, in One Page (then go deeper)
summary: A managed identity is the answer to 'how does software running on Azure authenticate without storing a single secret?' This page places it in the identity hierarchy and hands you off to the dedicated Managed Identity lane for the deep mechanics.
analogy: A service principal with a client secret is like a contractor who carries a copy of the building key in their wallet (losable, copyable). A managed identity is a badge the building itself issues and revokes automatically — the worker never holds a key, and there's nothing to drop in the parking lot.
diagram:
flowchart TD
  SP[Service principal\n+ secret you manage] -->|Azure manages it for you| MI[Managed identity]
  MI --> Sys[System-assigned\ntied to one resource]
  MI --> User[User-assigned\nstandalone, shareable]
  MI --> NoSecret[No secret to store or rotate]
  NoSecret --> Token[Azure injects tokens at runtime]
  Token --> Deep[See the dedicated Managed Identity lane]
body:
## First principles

The previous lesson left us with a sharp problem. A service principal needs a credential — a secret or certificate — to prove who it is. But a secret is a thing that must be *stored somewhere*, and anything stored can be leaked, committed to Git, baked into an image, or simply forgotten until it expires and breaks production. For software running **on Azure**, can we make the secret disappear entirely?

Yes. That's a **managed identity**.

## Why it exists

A managed identity is a **special kind of service principal whose credentials Azure creates, rotates, and protects for you** — and never shows you. Your code on a VM, App Service, Function, or container simply *asks the platform* for a token, and Azure hands one over because it already knows which identity that resource carries. There is no secret in your config, no rotation reminder, nothing to leak. It is the cleanest expression of Zero Trust for Azure-hosted workloads: strong identity, zero stored credentials.

In the identity hierarchy you've been building, it slots in neatly:

- **User / group** — humans.
- **Service principal** — software, *you* manage its credential.
- **Managed identity** — software *on Azure*, *Azure* manages its credential.

## How it works (the one-page version)

- There are **two flavors**. A **system-assigned** managed identity is enabled on one resource and shares its lifecycle (deleted when the resource is). A **user-assigned** managed identity is a standalone resource you can attach to many workloads and that outlives any one of them.
- Under the hood it's still a service principal in your tenant, so it **gets RBAC roles at a scope** exactly like any other identity. Grant it Reader on a storage account, and it can read — nothing more.
- Your code uses a credential helper (e.g. `DefaultAzureCredential` / `ManagedIdentityCredential`) that transparently fetches a token from the platform's local identity endpoint. Same code runs locally (as you) and in production (as the managed identity).

```bash
# Turn on a system-assigned identity for a web app, then grant it a tight role.
az webapp identity assign --name <app> --resource-group <rg>
az role assignment create \
  --assignee <principal-id> \
  --role "Storage Blob Data Reader" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/<rg>/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/<acct>"
```

## Go deeper — the dedicated lane

This page is deliberately a *bridge*, not the whole story. Managed identity has its own rich lane in this bootcamp — the **🔐 Managed Identity / Keyless Auth** lane (`foundry-auth`) — which covers the internals properly: system- vs user-assigned trade-offs, the IMDS token endpoint, `DefaultAzureCredential`'s dev-to-prod credential chain, granting access, workload identity federation, troubleshooting 403s, and best practices. **Don't re-learn it here — go there.** The *related* links on this page point straight into it.

## Gotchas

- **Managed identity only works for workloads running on (or federated into) Azure.** A laptop script or an on-prem server can't have one — those use service principals or federated credentials. Managed identity is the *on-Azure* answer.
- **Identity ≠ authorization.** Enabling a managed identity gives the resource an identity, but it can do *nothing* until you assign it an RBAC role at a scope. "I enabled the identity and still get 403" almost always means you forgot the role assignment (or it hasn't propagated yet).
- **System- vs user-assigned is a real design choice.** Pick system-assigned for a single, simple workload; user-assigned when identity must be shared, pre-created, or survive resource replacement. The dedicated lane covers this in depth.
- **Don't duplicate the deep lane in your head.** Treat this as the map pin; the territory is the `foundry-auth` lane.
teachItBack: A managed identity is a service principal whose secret Azure creates, rotates, and hides for you, so software running on Azure can authenticate with nothing stored to leak. It comes in system-assigned (tied to one resource) and user-assigned (standalone and shareable) flavors, and like any identity it does nothing until you grant it an RBAC role at a scope. It only applies to Azure-hosted or federated workloads — and the full mechanics live in the dedicated Managed Identity lane, which this page intentionally hands you off to rather than repeating.

glossary.definition: A service principal whose credentials Azure fully manages and never exposes, used by Azure-hosted workloads to authenticate without stored secrets.
A managed identity enabled on a single resource and deleted along with it.
A standalone managed identity resource that can be attached to many workloads and has its own lifecycle.
Authenticating without any stored secret or key, by having the platform issue tokens to a trusted identity.
